questions.question: What is Node.js according to the official introduction?
options: An open-source, cross-platform JavaScript runtime environment
A browser-only JavaScript framework for building UIs
A database engine for document storage
A package manager that replaces npm
questions.explanation: Node.js is described in the official introduction as an open-source and cross-platform JavaScript runtime environment.

questions.question: Which JavaScript engine does Node.js run?
options: SpiderMonkey
JavaScriptCore
V8
Hermes
questions.explanation: The official Node.js introduction states that Node.js runs the V8 JavaScript engine, which is also used by Google Chrome.

questions.question: Why can Node.js handle many concurrent connections with a single process?
options: Because it starts a dedicated Java thread per request
Because it uses asynchronous I/O primitives and avoids blocking by default
Because every callback runs on a separate CPU core automatically
Because the event loop executes all work in parallel JavaScript threads
questions.explanation: The introduction explains that Node.js provides asynchronous I/O primitives so JavaScript code does not block while waiting for network, filesystem, or database work.

questions.question: Which statement best matches the official description of a Node.js app?
options: It creates a new thread for every incoming request
It runs in a single process without creating a new thread for every request
It can only process one connection at a time
It requires worker threads for any I/O
questions.explanation: The official introduction says a Node.js app runs in a single process, without creating a new thread for every request.

questions.question: In the official Hello World example, which built-in module is imported to create the server?
options: node:net
node:http
node:stream
node:process
questions.explanation: The introductory example imports createServer from node:http to build a simple HTTP server.

questions.question: What does http.createServer() return in the official example?
options: A Promise that resolves to a port number
A new HTTP server instance
A readable stream containing the request body
A string URL that can be opened in the browser
questions.explanation: The introduction states that createServer() creates a new HTTP server and returns it.

questions.question: Which two objects are passed to the request handler of the Hello World server?
options: A path string and a status code
A request object and a response object
A socket object and a callback
A Buffer and an EventEmitter
questions.explanation: The docs explain that the request event provides a request object and a response object to handle the HTTP call.

questions.question: What is res.end() used for in the basic HTTP server example?
options: To close the response and optionally send the response body
To restart the event loop after a request finishes
To flush Node.js module cache
To terminate the entire process after one request
questions.explanation: In the introduction, res.end('Hello World') closes the response and sends the content back to the client.

questions.question: What is the event loop in Node.js?
options: A background compiler that transforms JavaScript into bytecode
A scheduling mechanism that lets Node.js perform non-blocking I/O
A cache for already executed callbacks
A queue that stores only timer callbacks
questions.explanation: The event loop guide defines it as the mechanism that allows Node.js to perform non-blocking I/O by offloading work when possible and processing callbacks later.

questions.question: Which event loop phase runs setImmediate() callbacks?
options: timers
poll
check
close callbacks
questions.explanation: The event loop guide says that setImmediate() callbacks are invoked in the check phase.

questions.question: Which event loop phase handles callbacks scheduled by setTimeout() and setInterval()?
options: timers
pending callbacks
check
close callbacks
questions.explanation: The event loop guide lists timers as the phase that executes callbacks scheduled by setTimeout() and setInterval().

questions.question: How does the official guide describe process.nextTick() relative to the event loop?
options: It is technically not part of the event loop and runs before the loop continues
It runs only after the close callbacks phase
It is the same API as setImmediate() with an older name
It always waits until at least 1 ms has passed
questions.explanation: The guide explicitly notes that process.nextTick() is not technically part of the event loop. Its queue is drained after the current operation completes and before the event loop continues.

questions.question: What is a known risk of recursively calling process.nextTick()?
options: It can starve I/O by preventing the loop from reaching the poll phase
It disables all timers for the lifetime of the process
It converts synchronous code into worker threads
It clears the microtask queue automatically
questions.explanation: The official guide warns that recursive process.nextTick() usage can starve I/O because Node keeps draining the next tick queue before continuing the event loop.

questions.question: What does the guide generally recommend using in most cases because it is easier to reason about?
options: process.nextTick()
setImmediate()
setInterval()
process.exit()
questions.explanation: The event loop guide says developers should generally use setImmediate() because it is easier to reason about than process.nextTick().

questions.question: Inside an I/O cycle, which is guaranteed to run first according to the event loop guide?
options: setTimeout(fn, 0)
setImmediate(fn)
Neither one can ever run first
The one registered second
questions.explanation: The guide explains that when both are scheduled inside an I/O callback, setImmediate() runs before timers such as setTimeout(..., 0).

questions.question: What class is exposed by the node:events module for working with event emitters?
options: EventTarget
EventEmitter
AsyncResource
Readable
questions.explanation: The events documentation states that the EventEmitter class is defined and exposed by the node:events module.

questions.question: How are EventEmitter listeners invoked when an event is emitted?
options: Asynchronously and in random order
Synchronously and in registration order
Only after the next timers phase
On a worker thread pool managed by libuv
questions.explanation: The events docs say EventEmitter calls all listeners synchronously in the order in which they were registered.

questions.question: What happens if an EventEmitter emits an 'error' event without an error listener?
options: The event is ignored
The error is thrown, a stack trace is printed, and the process exits
Node.js retries the event once automatically
The error is always converted into a warning
questions.explanation: The docs treat 'error' as a special event. If no listener is registered, the error is thrown and the process exits.

questions.question: What does emitter.once(eventName, listener) do?
options: Registers a listener that runs only the first time the event occurs
Registers a listener that runs once per event loop phase
Registers a listener that runs only after emitter.emit() returns false
Registers a listener that blocks other listeners
questions.explanation: The events module documents once() as adding a listener that is removed automatically after its first invocation.

questions.question: What default warning threshold do EventEmitter instances use for listeners on a single event?
questions.explanation: By default, EventEmitter warns when more than 10 listeners are added for a single event, helping surface possible memory leaks.

questions.question: Which list correctly names the four fundamental stream types in Node.js?
options: Readable, Writable, Duplex, Transform
Input, Output, Buffered, Reactive
Request, Response, File, Socket
Queue, Pipe, Event, Chunk
questions.explanation: The stream docs list four fundamental types: Readable, Writable, Duplex, and Transform.

questions.question: What mode do Readable streams begin in by default?
options: Flowing mode
Paused mode
Object mode
Closed mode
questions.explanation: The stream docs say all Readable streams begin in paused mode.

questions.question: Which action switches a Readable stream into flowing mode?
options: Calling stream.pause()
Adding a 'data' listener
Reading require.cache
Calling clearTimeout()
questions.explanation: A Readable switches into flowing mode when you add a 'data' listener, call resume(), or pipe it to a Writable stream.

questions.question: What does objectMode mean for a stream?
options: The stream can carry arbitrary JavaScript values instead of only strings or binary chunks
The stream automatically serializes objects to JSON strings
The stream is limited to plain objects and rejects arrays
The stream can only be used with HTTP requests
questions.explanation: In object mode, streams can work with JavaScript values other than the usual string, Buffer, TypedArray, or DataView chunks.

questions.question: What is stream.pipeline() primarily used for?
options: To connect streams and handle completion or failure as one operation
To convert any event emitter into a child process
To persist the require() cache to disk
To replace setImmediate() with Promises
questions.explanation: The stream documentation presents pipeline() as the utility for composing streams together and getting a single completion or error signal.

questions.question: What does writable.write(chunk) return false mean?
options: The chunk was rejected permanently
More writes should pause until the 'drain' event is emitted
The stream has already emitted 'finish'
The callback will never run
questions.explanation: The writable stream docs say that a false return value indicates backpressure and writes should pause until 'drain' fires.

questions.question: What is Buffer in Node.js?
options: A fixed-length sequence of bytes used for binary data
A Promise-based wrapper around streams
A special timer queue for network operations
A replacement for Uint8Array
questions.explanation: The buffer docs define Buffer objects as fixed-length byte sequences used to represent binary data.

questions.question: How does the official documentation describe Buffer relative to TypedArray?
options: Buffer is unrelated to TypedArray and uses a separate binary model
Buffer is a subclass of Uint8Array
Buffer can only wrap strings, not bytes
Buffer is deprecated in favor of Array
questions.explanation: The docs say Buffer extends JavaScript's Uint8Array and adds Node-specific binary data methods.

questions.question: Which API creates a zero-filled buffer and is the safer default?
options: Buffer.alloc(size)
Buffer.allocUnsafe(size)
new Buffer(size)
Buffer.from(size)
questions.explanation: Buffer.alloc() returns an initialized buffer. Buffer.allocUnsafe() is faster but may expose old memory until overwritten.

questions.question: What is important about Buffer.from(arrayBuffer)?
options: It always converts the data to UTF-8 text
It creates a copy and never shares memory
It can share the same underlying memory as the ArrayBuffer
It only works for zero-length ArrayBuffers
questions.explanation: The docs explain that Buffer.from(arrayBuffer) creates a Buffer view over the ArrayBuffer, sharing the same underlying memory.

questions.question: What does Buffer.byteLength(string, encoding) measure?
options: The number of JavaScript characters only
The number of bytes required for the value in the given encoding
The number of Unicode code points after normalization
The size of the V8 heap currently used by the string
questions.explanation: The buffer docs use Buffer.byteLength() to show that the byte length of a string can differ from its character count depending on encoding.

questions.question: Which two module systems does Node.js support?
options: CommonJS and ECMAScript modules
AMD and UMD only
RequireJS and SystemJS
JSON modules and CSS modules only
questions.explanation: The modules docs say Node.js supports both CommonJS modules and ECMAScript modules.

questions.question: What is true about require() and import() in Node.js?
options: Both always use the ECMAScript module loader
require() uses the CommonJS loader and import() uses the ECMAScript module loader
require() works only for built-in modules, not local files
import() can only load JSON files
questions.explanation: The official modules docs state that require() uses the CommonJS loader, while import() uses the ECMAScript module loader.

questions.question: In CommonJS, what does require.main === module usually indicate?
options: The file was loaded from node_modules
The file is running directly as the entry script
The file exports an EventEmitter
The file is using ECMAScript module syntax
questions.explanation: The modules docs explain that require.main is set to the module of the file run directly by Node.js, so require.main === module is a common direct-execution check.

questions.question: What does __filename contain in a CommonJS module?
options: Only the base file name without a path
The absolute path of the current module file, with symlinks resolved
The path to the nearest package.json
The name of the main entry file, even from dependencies
questions.explanation: The modules docs define __filename as the current module file's absolute path with symlinks resolved.

questions.question: What does __dirname contain in a CommonJS module?
options: The process working directory
The path.dirname(__filename) of the current module
The directory of the main entry script only
The nearest node_modules folder
questions.explanation: The modules docs say __dirname is the directory name of the current module and matches path.dirname(__filename).

questions.question: How does CommonJS module caching work in Node.js?
options: Modules are re-executed on every require() call
Modules are cached after the first load based on resolved filename
Only built-in modules are cached
Caching works only for JSON files
questions.explanation: The caching section states that modules are cached after the first time they are loaded, keyed by their resolved filename.

questions.question: Why might require('node:fs') be preferred over require('fs') in some cases?
options: It bypasses userland cache shadowing and always refers to the built-in module
It enables streaming mode automatically
It loads the module asynchronously
It switches the file system API to promise-only behavior
questions.explanation: The modules docs note that the node: prefix refers directly to the built-in module and bypasses require.cache shadowing.

questions.question: What can happen during circular CommonJS dependencies?
options: Node.js throws immediately on the first cycle
A module can receive an unfinished copy of another module's exports
The second module always replaces the first module's exports permanently
Circular imports work only for .json files
questions.explanation: The cycles section explains that to avoid infinite recursion, Node.js may return a partially initialized exports object during a circular require().

questions.question: What does require.resolve(request) do?
options: Loads the module and returns its exports
Returns the resolved filename without loading the module
Clears the module from the cache
Converts CommonJS to ECMAScript module syntax
questions.explanation: The modules docs describe require.resolve() as using Node's module resolution machinery to find the filename without loading the module.

questions.question: What is the main characteristic of child_process.spawn()?
options: It always runs the command in a shell and buffers all output
It spawns a process asynchronously without blocking the event loop
It only works for Node.js scripts
It is the synchronous child-process API
questions.explanation: The child_process docs say spawn() creates subprocesses asynchronously, without blocking the Node.js event loop.

questions.question: Which statement about child_process.exec() is correct?
options: It runs without a shell by default and streams output directly
It spawns a shell and buffers stdout and stderr
It is only available in worker threads
It cannot be aborted with AbortSignal
questions.explanation: The exec() docs explain that it spawns a shell, executes the command inside it, and buffers the output.

questions.question: Why might child_process.execFile() be preferable to exec() for running a known executable?
options: It does not spawn a shell by default and is typically more efficient
It automatically enables IPC between processes
It returns a Readable stream instead of a ChildProcess
It runs synchronously, so error handling is simpler
questions.explanation: The docs say execFile() runs the executable directly without spawning a shell by default, making it more efficient in many cases.

questions.question: What is special about child_process.fork()?
options: It clones the current V8 heap into a worker thread
It is a special case of spawn() for new Node.js processes with IPC
It can only execute shell built-ins
It always shares memory with the parent process
questions.explanation: The fork() docs describe it as a special case of spawn() used to launch a new Node.js process with an IPC communication channel.

questions.question: What is true about execSync(), execFileSync(), and spawnSync()?
options: They are promise-based and non-blocking
They block the Node.js event loop until the subprocess exits
They can only be used during process startup
They are aliases for process.nextTick()
questions.explanation: The synchronous child_process methods block the event loop until the child process completes, which can be useful for scripts but costly in servers.

questions.question: What is the difference between the ChildProcess 'exit' and 'close' events?
options: 'close' always happens before 'exit'
'exit' reports process end, while 'close' waits until stdio streams are closed
They are exact aliases and always fire together
'exit' is only for Windows and 'close' is only for Unix
questions.explanation: The docs distinguish them: 'exit' fires when the process ends, while 'close' fires after the process ends and its stdio streams have also closed.

questions.question: When does a ChildProcess emit an 'error' event?
options: Only when stderr receives text
When spawning fails, killing fails, sending a message fails, or an abort signal aborts the process
Only when exit code is 0
Only after the 'close' event
questions.explanation: The child_process docs list several cases for 'error', including spawn failures, kill failures, send failures, and aborts.

questions.question: What does process.argv contain?
options: Only environment variables used by the process
An array of command-line arguments, including the Node executable path and entry script
Only flags passed after --inspect
A map of parsed named options
questions.explanation: The process docs state that process.argv returns an array of command-line arguments, beginning with process.execPath and usually the entry script path.

questions.question: What does process.cwd() return?
options: The absolute path of the current working directory
The directory of the current module
The location of the Node.js executable
The user's home directory
questions.explanation: The process documentation defines process.cwd() as the current working directory of the process.

questions.question: What is process.env?
options: A read-only snapshot of V8 flags
An object containing the user environment variables
A list of loaded native add-ons
A buffer of process warnings
questions.explanation: The process docs describe process.env as the object containing the user environment.

questions.question: What is the official guidance around process.exit() in most applications?
options: Call it after every console.log() to flush output
Avoid calling it unless necessary; prefer setting process.exitCode and letting the process exit naturally
Use it inside every catch block
It is required to end a Node.js HTTP server
questions.explanation: The process docs warn that process.exit() can terminate too quickly and truncate pending I/O. In many cases, setting process.exitCode is safer.

questions.question: What limitation applies to work done inside a process 'exit' event handler?
options: Only synchronous work will run reliably
Only Promise-based code is allowed
Only writes to stderr are allowed
The handler may start new timers that finish before shutdown
questions.explanation: The docs state that once 'exit' runs, the process is about to terminate, so only synchronous operations should be performed.

questions.question: What makes the 'beforeExit' event different from 'exit'?
options: beforeExit can schedule more asynchronous work and keep the process alive
beforeExit always fires after process.exit()
beforeExit is emitted only in worker threads
beforeExit cannot read process.exitCode
questions.explanation: The process docs explain that 'beforeExit' is emitted when the event loop becomes empty, and a listener can schedule more async work, causing the process to continue.

questions.question: What is Node.js default behavior for an uncaught exception if you do not override it?
options: Retry the failing callback once
Print the stack trace to stderr and exit with code 1
Convert the exception into an 'error' event on process.stdout
Ignore it if a warning handler exists
questions.explanation: The process docs say uncaught exceptions are printed to stderr and cause the process to exit with code 1 by default.

questions.question: What does process.memoryUsage() report?
options: Only V8 heap usage in megabytes
A detailed memory usage object including rss, heapTotal, heapUsed, external, and arrayBuffers
Only the memory consumed by Buffers
Only memory used by child processes
questions.explanation: The process docs show process.memoryUsage() returning an object with rss, heapTotal, heapUsed, external, and arrayBuffers.

questions.question: What happens when you add a listener for SIGINT or SIGTERM on non-Windows platforms?
options: Node.js keeps the default exit behavior unchanged
The default behavior is removed, so Node.js no longer exits automatically for that signal
The process ignores the signal completely and cannot observe it
All uncaught exceptions are converted into that signal
questions.explanation: The signal docs explain that installing listeners for SIGINT or SIGTERM removes Node.js's default behavior for those signals.

questions.question: What does setTimeout(callback, delay) guarantee?
options: The callback runs at exactly delay milliseconds
The callback runs no earlier than the delay, but actual timing can be later
The callback always runs before any I/O callback
The callback is scheduled in the check phase
questions.explanation: The timers docs say Node.js makes no guarantee of exact timing. The callback is invoked as close as possible after the threshold has passed.

questions.question: What does timeout.unref() do for a scheduled timer?
options: It cancels the timer immediately
It prevents the timer from keeping the event loop alive
It converts the timer into a Promise
It moves the timer into the microtask queue
questions.explanation: The timers docs say unref() means the timer will not require the event loop to remain active; the process may exit before it fires if nothing else is pending.

questions.question: What does the timers/promises API provide?
options: Browser-only timer shims for frontend code
Promise-based alternatives to timer functions like setTimeout() and setImmediate()
A replacement for child_process timers
A scheduler that works only in ECMAScript modules
questions.explanation: The timers/promises docs present promise-returning alternatives such as setTimeout(), setImmediate(), and setInterval().

questions.question: How can promise-based timers like timersPromises.setTimeout() be canceled?
options: By deleting require.cache entries
By passing an AbortSignal and aborting it
By emitting an 'error' event on process
By calling process.exitCode = 1
questions.explanation: The timers docs show that the promise-based timer APIs accept an AbortSignal, and aborting it rejects the promise with an AbortError.
